title: Service Integration Architecture
content: ## What Is This?
This document describes how NxGN Actions integrates with the broader NxGN
platform. Actions is both a **consumer** of Identity (for authentication and
authorization) and a **provider** of action management services to product
services (Capstone, Guardian, Pulse).
## Token Flow Patterns
Three patterns cover all service-to-service authentication scenarios:
### 1. User-Context Token Forwarding
**Pattern:** Product service forwards the user's JWT to the Actions API.
Browser → Product BFF (cookie auth) → Product API (JWT) → Actions API (same JWT)
The Actions API validates the JWT independently using Identity's JWKS endpoint.
No token exchange or re-authentication occurs — the original user's JWT travels
through the call chain.
**When to use:** Any call where Actions needs to know *which user* is acting
(e.g., Capstone API calling Actions to create an action on behalf of a user).
### 2. Client Credentials (M2M)
**Pattern:** Service authenticates as itself using `client_credentials` grant.
Product Service → Identity /connect/token → M2M JWT
Product Service → Actions API (M2M JWT in Authorization header)
The M2M token has `sub` set to the `client_id`. There is no user context —
tenant context is conveyed in the request body. Actions records the originating
`client_id` as `origin_system` on created actions.
**When to use:** Autonomous service-to-service operations with no user
involvement (e.g., Guardian automatically creating corrective actions from
inspection findings).
### 3. BFF Routes to Actions API
**Pattern:** Actions BFF proxies browser requests to the Actions API.
Browser → Actions BFF /api/actions/* → Actions API (JWT in header)
The BFF translates the session cookie to a JWT and routes to the Actions API.
Product BFFs can also proxy directly to Actions if they need embedded action
management within their own UI.
## Startup Bootstrap
When the Actions API starts, it performs an idempotent bootstrap sequence
against Identity using its M2M client:
1. **Acquire M2M token** via `client_credentials` grant.
2. **Register permission types** — 22 permissions (idempotent upsert).
3. **Register role templates** — 4 default templates that Identity materializes
into org-scoped roles when a Platform Admin enables Actions for an
organization.
4. **Sync tenant list** — Identity returns only organizations with active
Actions entitlements. Actions upserts its `TenantConfiguration` table.
**On-demand tenant creation:** If a request arrives for an unknown `tenant_id`,
Actions creates a placeholder row and backfills from Identity on the next sync.
**Failure resilience:** Startup retries with exponential backoff. If Identity is
unavailable, Actions starts with existing tenant configuration and syncs when
Identity becomes available.
## Client Registry
Actions has the following OIDC clients registered in Identity:
| Client ID | Type | Purpose |
| `nxgn.actions.web` | Confidential | Portal web BFF (authorization_code + refresh_token) |
| `nxgn.actions.cli` | Public | CLI tool (authorization_code + device_code) |
| `nxgn.actions.mcp` | Public | MCP server (authorization_code) |
| `nxgn.actions.service` | Confidential | M2M service-to-service (client_credentials) |
Product services that call Actions via M2M use their own service clients:
| Client ID | Purpose |
| `capstone-api-svc` | Capstone automated action creation |
| `guardian-api-svc` | Guardian server-side action creation |
| `pulse-api-svc` | Pulse automated action creation |
## JWT Validation
The Actions API validates JWTs **offline** via Identity's JWKS endpoint. There
are zero calls to an introspection endpoint during normal request processing.
**Why offline:** Eliminates Identity as a runtime dependency for every API call.
If Identity is temporarily unavailable, Actions continues operating with cached
JWKS keys.
**Key rotation:** Keys refresh only on signature validation failure (standard
OIDC key rotation protocol).
### SecurityEpoch Validation
User JWTs carry a `security_epoch` claim — an integer incremented on security
events (password change, force logout). Actions checks this against a cached
value on each request. Tokens issued before a security event are rejected with
401 (near-real-time token revocation without requiring Identity to be online).
### API Key Authentication
In addition to JWT Bearer authentication, Actions supports API key
authentication. API keys are validated against Identity's validation endpoint.
When Identity is unavailable, API key validation fails with 503 (unlike JWT
validation which uses cached JWKS).
## Notification Service Integration
> **Status: Post-Baseline.** Phase 1 records escalation audit events only.
Actions will integrate with the NxGN Notification Service for:
- Escalation notifications when actions breach tier thresholds
- Deadline warning alerts for approaching regulatory deadlines
- Consultation request notifications to consulted workers
The integration pattern will be fire-and-forget via the Notification Service API
using the M2M client (`nxgn.actions.service`). Notification delivery failures
must not block action operations — the audit trail records the event regardless.
## Shared Integration Packages (Planned)
> **Status: Post-Baseline.** Planned after the core platform stabilizes.
Shared NuGet packages will extract common security infrastructure:
| Package | Purpose |
| `NxGN.Auth.BFF` | BFF session middleware |
| `NxGN.Auth.ResourceServer` | JWT validation middleware (issuer, audience, RFC 9068) |
| `NxGN.Auth.Cli` | CLI OAuth flow (authorization code + PKCE, device code) |
| `NxGN.Auth.Mcp` | MCP OAuth flow |
**Why:** Centralizes security fixes across products, ensures consistent token
validation, and eliminates per-product copy-paste of BFF middleware setup.
